does: This paper introduces MOS-RMBench, a unified benchmark that converts diverse Mean Opinion Score datasets into pairwise preference comparisons for speech quality assessment. The authors systematically construct and evaluate three reward modeling paradigms—scalar, semi-scalar, and generative reward models—to predict which speech sample users would prefer. Through comprehensive experiments, they demonstrate that scalar reward models achieve the strongest performance at exceeding 74% accuracy, but reveal critical limitations: models perform substantially worse on synthetic speech compared to human speech, and all approaches struggle with preference pairs exhibiting minimal MOS differences, indicating fundamental challenges in fine-grained quality discrimination.
connects: This work extends prior research on speech quality assessment beyond traditional human subjective rating limitations. It builds on decades of MOS evaluation methodology in speech processing while incorporating recent advances in preference-based reward modeling from reinforcement learning and language model alignment. The reformulation into preference comparisons responds to known reproducibility issues in subjective evaluation and connects to broader efforts in the community to develop automated, scalable quality assessment metrics. The exploration of different reward modeling architectures draws from contemporary work on learning from preference data, adapting these techniques specifically to the speech domain where synthetic versus natural quality gaps remain significant challenges.
enables: This benchmark and analysis enable future development of more robust automated speech quality metrics that could reduce reliance on expensive human evaluation. The identification of specific failure modes—particularly on synthetic speech and fine-grained distinctions—points toward targeted improvements in reward model architectures and training procedures. The preference-based formulation supports integration with preference optimization methods for speech generation, potentially enabling better direct optimization of model outputs toward human preferences. The systematic evaluation framework facilitates future research into hybrid approaches combining different reward modeling paradigms and investigation into why synthetic speech quality assessment remains fundamentally more difficult than human speech evaluation.
